title: Syndrome resampling enhances quantum error correction thresholds
authors: Luis Colmenarez, Áron Márton, Markus Müller
abstract: Quantum error correction (QEC) enables fault-tolerant quantum computation but requires operating quantum hardware at physical error rates below code-dependent thresholds, which remains challenging for current devices. We introduce syndrome resampling, a general method that increases QEC thresholds of any decoder and suppresses logical errors without additional hardware, decoding modifications, or code-specific assumptions beyond syndrome statistics. The method exploits the fact that syndromes with low probability are likely to lead to logical failure, therefore biasing syndrome averages towards most likely syndromes effectively increases logical fidelities. We establish a direct connection between the Rényi coherent information (RCI) and powers of the syndrome probability distribution, showing that resampling syndromes according to these powers combined with maximum likelihood decoding (MLD) realizes a family of optimal thresholds associated with phase transitions in the RCI. Numerical simulations of surface codes demonstrate that syndrome resampling substantially increases thresholds for both optimal and suboptimal decoders and reduces logical error rates by up to four orders of magnitude in experimentally relevant regimes. We further show that syndrome resampling can be effectively implemented from finite data and combined with decoding-based post-selection to achieve additional gains. Finally, applying the method to existing experimental QEC data yields up to two orders of magnitude reduction in logical error rates without requiring additional measurements. Our results provide a practical and decoder-agnostic route to improved logical fidelities in near-term QEC experiments.
summary: This paper presents a new method called syndrome resampling that improves quantum error correction performance by reweighting syndrome data. It works for any decoder and can significantly reduce logical error rates in both simulations and real experimental data without needing new hardware. The method effectively pushes the error correction threshold higher by focusing on the most probable error patterns.
main_problem: The difficulty of increasing quantum error correction (QEC) thresholds and reducing logical error rates without requiring additional hardware or complex, code-specific decoding modifications.
main_result: The introduction of syndrome resampling, which increases QEC thresholds and reduces logical error rates by up to four orders of magnitude in simulations and two orders of magnitude in experimental data.
method: A decoder-agnostic method called syndrome resampling that biases syndrome averages towards more likely syndromes by resampling according to powers of the syndrome probability distribution.
model_or_system: Quantum error correction codes, specifically surface codes (unrotated and rotated) and the toric code, subject to Pauli bit-flip noise.
key_observables: Logical error rate, syndrome probability distribution (SPD), Renyi Coherent Information (RCI), and threshold (p_th).
important_parameters: Resampling parameter alpha, physical error rate p, code distance d, and critical exponent nu.
main_assumptions: The noise model is assumed to be Pauli noise, and the method's efficiency is limited by the sampling cost required to estimate the syndrome probability distribution.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the reconstruction of the modified syndrome distribution; Figure 2 displays logical error rates vs. error rates for different alpha values and shows data collapse; Figure 3 compares various decoding strategies; Figure 4 shows the evolution of error rates and acceptance rates; Figure 6 shows finite-size scaling collapse for MWPM.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the syndrome resampling method, establishes its theoretical connection to Renyi Coherent Information and phase transitions, presents numerical simulations on surface codes, demonstrates implementation with finite data and combination with post-selection, and validates the method using existing experimental QEC data.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Numerical reconstruction of the modified syndrome distribution Qα(s) for the d = 5 unrotated surface code, p = 0.2 and α = 1, 2, 3. We consider 20 stabilizers, which makes a total of 220 possible syndromes. The index one denotes the trivial syndrome. The distribution becomes increasingly peaked around the more probable syndromes as α is increased.

FIG. 2. The logical error rate as a function of the bit-flip error rate in the vicinity of the respective threshold for (a) α = 1, (b) α = 2, and (c) α = 3. The logical error rates are shown for both MWPM and MLD decoding and for multiple code distances. The insets show the collapsed data for MLD, with the extracted threshold pth and critical exponent ν values listed in Tab. I. The dashed gray lines mark the corresponding RCI threshold values listed in Tab. I.

FIG. 3. Logical error rate for surface code for bit-flip noise error rate p = 0.08, 0.1, 0.12, 0.14, 0.16. We use MWPM only (blue), syndrome resampling (SR) (orange), complementary gap post-selection (CGPS)(green) and both SR and CGPS (red). For SR we set α = 2. The complementary gap confi- dence parameter is set to c = 2/5, 2/3, 1/2, 2/3 [73] for code distances d = 5, 6, 8, 9 respectively. The lowest logical error rate is always obtained by decoding using both strategies. The grey dashed line denotes the standard optimal threshold p ∼0.109.

FIG. 4. Logical error rate (top panels) and acceptance rate (bottom panels) of the resampling method under increasing number of samples N for p = 0.12 (left panels) and p = 0.14 (right panels). Post-selected (circles) and α = 2 (stars) resam- pled data are considered. The acceptance rate is calculated as the number of samples kept in independently sampled batches of size N. The post-selected error rate for d = 9 (not shown) is too low to be reliably computed with the present number of samples. Error bars are computed via bootstrapping and represent the 67% confidence interval.

FIG. 5. Logical error rate in the ˆZL1 ˆZL2 expectation value of the repetition code lattice surgery experiment of Ref. [5]. A surface-17 superconducting processor is initialized in a surface-code state, after which the central column of data qubits is measured out, yielding two entangled three-qubit repetition codes encoding a logical Bell pair. Here α = 1, 2, 3 denotes sampling over the probability distribution bQα (α = 1 denotes no-resampling) and decoding using MWPM. PS de- notes the expectation value discarding all non-trivial syn- dromes. The total number of samples used was 44268.
